lines.original: Jadaan pakki aale aa saanu neri da ni hunda
lines.translated: When the staunch ones are around, 'neri' doesn't happen to us.
lines.note: 'pakki aale' = staunch/loyal people; 'ni hunda' = 'doesn't happen' (see glossary). 'neri' is ambiguous regional slang (could imply rivalry, weakness, or something else), so it's left untranslated here.

lines.original: Sutta peya sher kade chhedi da ni hunda
lines.translated: A lion with a cigarette is never the type to be provoked.
lines.note: 'sutta peya' = with a cigarette / smoking; 'sher' = lion (metaphor for a tough man); 'ni hunda' per glossary = 'doesn't happen / isn't done' (here: 'isn't the type to be provoked').

lines.original: Kalla dekh ke bagana putt gheri da ni hunda
lines.translated: Running away when you see me alone, son — an encirclement doesn't happen.
lines.note: ‘putt’ is a vocative meaning ‘son’ (colloquial). ‘gheri’ means an encirclement/ambush; ‘ni hunda’ = ‘doesn't happen’. Here 'bagana' is interpreted as 'bhaagna' (to run away) based on context.

lines.original: Mai keha yaariyan ch jatta teri meri da ni hunda
lines.translated: I say, in friendship, jatta, there's no 'yours and mine'.
lines.note: 'jatta' = vocative 'jatt / bro' (see glossary). 'teri meri da ni hunda' means friendships aren't possessive—'there's no yours-and-mine.'

lines.original: 5-7 cheejan hun bikaneri pehli hove
lines.translated: Now let the first 5–7 things be Bikaneri.
lines.note: “Bikaneri” literally means someone or something from Bikaner (a city in Rajasthan). Here it’s ambiguous — could refer to a person, a style, or a quality tied to Bikaner.

lines.original: Dooji hove bande khaane tor jivein baili hove
lines.translated: Second, if it's another man, he breaks things like a bull.
lines.note: Literal reading: 'khaane tor' is ambiguous (could refer to 'breaking' or a manner of force); 'baili' likely relates to 'bail' (bull), implying brute force. I kept a conservative, literal sense rather than adding implied threats or specifics.

lines.original: Teeja chale naam pura gal saare faili hove
lines.translated: When the third one moves, the full name is spoken and all the talk spreads
lines.note: ‘Teeja’ literally means ‘the third (one)’ and is ambiguous here (third person, third strike, etc.). ‘Naam pura’ literally ‘full name’ can mean the actual name being said or a person's reputation; ‘gal saare faili hove’ means the news/gossip spreads everywhere.

lines.original: Aava pindo aiddan lagge biba jivein rally hove
lines.translated: When she comes to the village, girl, it's like a rally.
lines.note: 'biba' = girl (vocative). 'pindo' = to the village; 'jivein rally hove' implies she draws a crowd or seems like a parade/rally.

lines.original: Kade time si kehndi si ki aa palle palle jatta
lines.translated: There were times she used to say, 'Come close, come close, bro.'
lines.note: 'jatta' = jatt / bro / young man; a familiar/vocative address.

lines.original: Kehndi dekh ke ferrari kitthe challe kalle jatta
lines.translated: She says, after seeing it, where will you take the Ferrari alone, jatta
lines.transliteration: ਕਹਿੰਦੀ ਦੇਖ ਕੇ ਫ਼ੇਰਾਰੀ ਕਿੱਥੇ ਚੱਲੇ ਕੱਲੇ ਜੱਟਾ
lines.note: jatta: vocative 'jatt / bro / young man' (see glossary).

lines.original: Jinne opp ne saare ve terе thalle thalle jatta
lines.translated: All your opps are under you, jatt
lines.note: ‘opps’ = opponents/enemies (slang). ‘jatt’ used vocatively — see glossary meaning: jatt / bro / young man.

lines.original: Naddi dekh kе chadhayi kehndi balle balle jatta
lines.translated: Seeing the river, she says "climb up," cheering "balle balle, jatta."
lines.transliteration: ਨਦੀ ਦੇਖ ਕੇ ਚੜ੍ਹਾਈ ਕਹਿੰਦੀ ਬੱਲੇ ਬੱਲੇ ਜੱਟਾ
lines.note: “balle balle” is a celebratory exclamation; “jatta” is a vocative address (jatt / bro). “chadhayi” is taken literally as ‘tell/encourage to climb/get on.’

lines.original: 5-7 cheeja hon pehli kaali ghodi hove
lines.translated: There should be five to seven things — the first, a black mare.

lines.original: Dooji beeba farm te cheeneyan di jodi hove
lines.translated: Second, may there be a pair of Chinese girls on the farm
lines.note: 'beeba' = 'girl' (see glossary). 'cheeneyan' is literally 'Chinese women' — kept literal; could be colloquial reference to foreign/Chinese girls.

lines.original: Teeji hove anakh ve maaya chahe thodi hove
lines.translated: Let the third be pride, even if the wealth is little.
lines.note: Teeji = 'third' (as in the third item in a list). 'Anakh' means pride/self-respect (see glossary). 'Maaya' refers to wealth/material possessions.

lines.original: Chadh ke aayi nu bhajj dooni ghar modi hove
lines.translated: Having climbed up, she ran off; may the house be turned.
lines.note: The line is dialectal/ambiguous. 'bhajj' means 'ran (off)'; 'dooni' is unclear here (likely emphatic). 'modi hove' literally 'may (the) house be turned' — possibly meaning 'crooked' or 'ruined'. Kept a conservative, literal reading.

lines.original: Saade jigre nu dekhi beeba matta nu na dekhi
lines.translated: Look at our courage; don't look at the girl's matta
lines.note: Used glossary: 'jigre' = courage/guts, 'beeba' = girl. The word 'matta' is ambiguous in this line (dialectal); literal form kept as 'matta' because its precise meaning (e.g., face, mother, or another local term) is unclear from context.

lines.original: Takkaan nu naa dekhi ehna satta nu na dekhi
lines.translated: Don't mind the blows, don't mind their power.
lines.note: ‘Takkaan’ is ambiguous here; rendered conservatively as 'blows/strikes'. ‘Satta’ means 'power/authority'.

lines.original: Jor gaaddaran chon hunda beeba chatta nu na dekhi
lines.translated: Strength comes from traitors, girl — don't look at chatta
lines.note: 'beeba' = girl / dear girl (see glossary). 'chatta' is ambiguous here, so it is left untranslated literally.

lines.original: Sirr ghum jau rakaane ehna akhan nu na dekhi
lines.translated: Turn your head away; don't look into these eyes.

lines.original: Panj saat cheejan hun pehli yaari pakki hove
lines.translated: Of five or seven things, first — friendship must be solid.
lines.note: ’Panj saat’ literally means ‘five-seven’ (listing items); ‘yaari’ = friendship/loyalty (priority here).

lines.original: Dooja paaya kurta te jeep thalle rakhi hove
lines.translated: Second, he wears a kurta and keeps a jeep parked below.

lines.original: Teeji hovi ohth ohdi ehna munda lucky hove
lines.translated: If a third one there were hers, these boys would be lucky.
lines.note: Literal reading: 'teeji' = 'third (one)', 'hovi' = conditional 'would be', 'ohth' = 'there', 'ehna' = 'these'. Translation is conservative because the line is ambiguous about who/what 'third' refers to.

lines.original: Latta khich khich jaanda jor laala thakki hove
lines.translated: She keeps tugging and tugging; pulling hard, she gets tired.
lines.note: Latta khich khich literally means repeatedly tugging (often at clothing or playfully). 'Jor' = force; 'laala' here is ambiguous but read as an intensifier meaning 'with strength' — so rendered 'pulling hard'. Translation kept conservative and literal.

lines.original: Fer ki karegi mile nitt ni je
lines.translated: So what will she do if she doesn't meet (him) every day?
lines.note: 'je' = 'if'; 'ni' here is a colloquial particle/negation. 'karegi' is feminine ('she'). The object of 'meet' is unstated in the original line.

lines.original: Dass main ki kara ni laga chitt ni je
lines.translated: Tell me what I should do if that doesn't work.
lines.note: The phrase 'laga chitt' is ambiguous here — 'chitt' can mean 'chit/note' or be a dialectal/slang term. 'ni' is negation and 'je' means 'if'. I rendered it conservatively as 'that' to preserve the conditional sense without assuming a specific meaning for 'chitt'.

lines.original: Mere sohniye dream kuch hor aithe
lines.translated: My dear, dreams are something else here

lines.original: Jor baahaan ch chahida jang jitt ni je
lines.translated: Strong arms are needed; the war won't be won.
lines.transliteration: Jor baahaan ch chahida, jang jitt ni je
lines.note: 'ni' is a colloquial negation ('isn't'/'won't'); 'je' here adds emphasis. Translation kept literal and conservative.

lines.original: Paanj saat cheejan pehla lv da leeda hove
lines.translated: Five or seven things — the first should be 'lv da leeda'.
lines.note: 'lv da leeda' is ambiguous: 'lv' might mean 'love', the brand 'LV', or 'level'; 'leeda' is unclear (could be a dialectal/slang form or 'leader'). Kept literal rather than guessing.

lines.original: Dooja kuch karne da andar koi keeda hove
lines.translated: Second: an inner urge (a compulsion) to do something.
lines.note: 'keeda' literally means 'bug' but here denotes an inner itch/compulsion or strong urge.

lines.original: Teeja hove yaari aisa jebo chahe peeda hove
lines.translated: Let the third be a friendship like that, even if there’s pain.
lines.note: yaari = friendship/loyalty (see glossary). 'jebo' here is interpreted conservatively as 'even if'; 'peeda' means 'pain'. Transliteration omitted because the original is already in Latin script.

lines.original: Jithe akhaan adh jaave sachi ehna cheeda hove
lines.translated: Where the eyes half-close, truly they'd be this much of a tease.
lines.transliteration: ਜਿੱਥੇ ਅੱਖਾਂ ਅੱਧ ਜਾਵੇ, ਸੱਚੀ ਇਹਨਾ ਛੇੜਾ ਹੋਵੇ
lines.note: sachi = 'truly'/'really' (emphatic). cheeda/cheda here read conservatively as 'tease' or 'teasing' (someone flirtatious or mischievous).

lines.original: Mere kurta hove te tera booti aala suit
lines.translated: If I've got my kurta on and you're in that booti-style suit

lines.original: Chete kari firre tu ghurale aala route
lines.translated: You keep remembering as you wander that winding route
lines.note: The word 'ghurale' is dialectal/ambiguous; rendered here as 'winding' (curvy/roundabout).

lines.original: Main keha mere naal chali jado jaave gedi route
lines.translated: I said, come with me when we go out on a joyride.
lines.note: 'gedi route' means a cruising/joyride route—driving around for fun or to show off.

lines.original: Dekhi dedh minute bich vajju dedh sau salute mai keha
lines.translated: I saw — within one and a half minutes I fired one hundred and fifty salutes, I said
lines.note: dedh = 'one and a half'. 'Salute' is kept literal; it likely refers to fired shots or celebratory salutes (literal or metaphorical).

lines.original: 5-7 cheejan hun pehli bebe khush hove
lines.translated: Now 5-7 things; first, the girl must be happy.
lines.note: ‘bebe’ is an affectionate term for a girl (see glossary entry 'biba'/'beeba').

lines.original: Dooji meri baapu di haye rabb valla mucch hove
lines.translated: Second, may my father, by God, have a moustache.
lines.note: ‘Haye rabb valla’ is an exclamatory oath invoking God; a moustache (mucch) is often a cultural symbol of masculine pride or honor in Punjabi context.

lines.original: Teeja saala area ch naam hove puchh hove
lines.translated: Third, there should be someone in the area with a name and respect.
lines.note: ‘saala’ is a colloquial/expletive intensifier here (not literal brother‑in‑law). ‘naam’ and ‘puchh’ refer to being known and having reputation/standing.

lines.original: Meri jaan jaavein mere yaaran nu na kuch hove
lines.translated: Even if I lose my life, may nothing happen to my friends.
lines.note: “Meri jaan jaavein” is a hyperbolic vow of sacrifice (may I lose my life). “Yaaran” means friends/close comrades; the line expresses protective loyalty.

lines.original: Ni main gaddiyan na thaalda barola jitho langga
lines.translated: I don't make my cars roar where I pass
lines.note: “Ni” is an emphatic/vocative particle common in Punjabi. “Gaddiyan” means cars. I interpreted “thaalda barola” conservatively as causing a loud noise or roar with the vehicles.

lines.original: Ni main maadeyaan li maada biba changeyaan layi changga
lines.translated: I'm bad for the bad ones; I'm good for the good girls.
lines.note: “Ni” is a filler/interjection (vocative). “biba” = girl (see glossary). “maadeyaan” is read literally as ‘the bad ones’—this line contrasts being bad to bad people and good to good girls; translation kept conservative and literal.

lines.original: Jaane neeli chhat aala dass ohto kahda sanga
lines.translated: Tell me — the one from the blue-roofed house, who was from there?

lines.original: Bas paanj saat cheejan ohto jyada naa mai manga mai keha
lines.translated: I only asked for five or seven things; I said not more than that.

lines.original: Panj saat cheejan pehla daang utte dera hove
lines.translated: Let five or seven things be placed first, up on the top

lines.original: Dooji akh baaj aali sher jeha jera hove
lines.translated: The second eye would be like a hawk's, like that of a lion.

lines.original: Teeja aida vedha hove kile jeda ghera hove
lines.translated: Third, one so strange, like a fort that's surrounded
lines.note: Vedha = strange/weird (dialect). Kile = fort; ghera = surrounded/encircled — the line compares someone to a fortified place that’s enclosed.

lines.original: 5 7 cheejan hun hor na bathera hove
lines.translated: Now just five or seven things—there shouldn't be many more.
lines.note: '5 7' is a colloquial way to say 'a few'.
